county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Butte County. Named for J.A. Harding, SD state senator. Most remote and least populated county in SD. Contains Slim Buttes and Cave Hills.
county_metadata.research_tips: Harding County Register of Deeds in Buffalo (SD) holds deed records from 1909. Harding County is SD's most remote and least populated county — local records are limited. BLM GLO homestead records are essential for tracing original settlers; many homesteads were abandoned during drought years. Harding County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. Cattle and sheep ranching dominated; livestock brand records at the SD Brand Board identify ranching families. The Harding County newspapers at SDSHS cover sparse community history. Butte County holds pre-1909 records. Slim Buttes battlefield (1876) is in the county — military records at NARA document this engagement during the Great Sioux War. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed county records. The county's extreme isolation means researchers should check regional newspapers and ranching organization records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1909 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1909. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Alexander Hughes, Dakota Territory legislator. Pierre (county seat) is the state capital of South Dakota. Located on the Missouri River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hughes County Register of Deeds in Pierre holds deed records from 1873. Pierre is the state capital — the South Dakota State Historical Society (SDSHS) and State Archives are located here, holding territorial and state government records, microfilmed county records, vital records, military records, and manuscript collections. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Hughes County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. The South Dakota State Library in Pierre has census microfilm and genealogical reference materials. The Pierre newspapers (Capital Journal, Pierre Daily Free Press) on microfilm at SDSHS document state capital politics and community life. Fort Pierre (across the Missouri) was a major fur trade post — American Fur Company records at the Missouri Historical Society (St. Louis) cover the earliest Euro-American presence. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1873 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county. Named for John Hutchinson. One of the earliest settled counties, with strong German-Russian (Mennonite, Hutterite) communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hutchinson County Register of Deeds in Olivet holds some of SD's oldest land records from 1862. As an original Dakota Territory county, early records may also be at SDSHS. German-Russian (Mennonite and Hutterite) settlers were predominant — Mennonite church records at the Center for Mennonite Brethren Studies (Fresno) and Mennonite Library and Archives (North Newton, KS) are essential. Hutterite colonies maintain their own genealogical records. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Hutchinson County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Freeman is a center of Mennonite culture — the Heritage Hall Museum and Archives in Freeman holds Mennonite and Hutterite family records. The Freeman Courier and Olivet newspapers are on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1862 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1862. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for James Hyde, early settler. Agricultural county in central SD. Highmore was called 'the center of the world' by promoters.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hyde County Register of Deeds in Highmore holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Hyde County Clerk of Courts in Highmore has probate, naturalization, and civil records. German and Scandinavian settlers predominated. The Highmore Herald newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Railroad colonization brought many settlers during the Great Dakota Boom (1878-1887). Lutheran and Catholic church records serve the primary ethnic communities. The county experienced significant population loss during drought and depression years. Oahe Dam (1948-1962) flooded Missouri River bottomlands in western Hyde County — pre-dam homestead records at BLM are important.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Stanley County. Located adjacent to Badlands National Park and Pine Ridge Reservation. Named for President Andrew Jackson. Previously known as Washabaugh County (separate entity merged 1983).
county_metadata.research_tips: Jackson County Register of Deeds in Kadoka holds deed records from 1914. The county borders Badlands National Park and Pine Ridge Reservation — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls and allotment records for Oglala Lakota families. Washabaugh County was merged into Jackson County in 1983; check both county names when searching records. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims. Jackson County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. The Kadoka Press newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Cattle ranching is the primary economy; livestock brand records identify ranching families. Stanley County holds pre-1914 records. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed county records. The Badlands area was homesteaded by optimistic settlers, many of whom ultimately failed due to arid conditions.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1914 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1914. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1883 from Aurora County. Named for H.A. Jerauld, Dakota Territory legislator. Home to the Shakespeare Garden in Wessington Springs.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jerauld County Register of Deeds in Wessington Springs holds deed records from 1883. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Jerauld County Clerk of Courts in Wessington Springs has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Scandinavian and German settlers predominated — Lutheran and Catholic church records are important sources. The Wessington Springs True Dakotan newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county's agricultural economy relied on wheat, corn, and livestock. Aurora County holds some pre-1883 records. The Wessington Springs area attracted artesian well drilling — early water records document settlement patterns.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1916 from Lyman County. Named for Jones County in Iowa. One of SD's least populated counties. Located along I-90.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jones County Register of Deeds in Murdo holds deed records from 1916. BLM GLO homestead records document original settlers. Jones County Clerk of Courts in Murdo has probate and civil records. Cattle ranching is the primary economy; livestock brand records at the SD Brand Board identify ranching families. The Murdo Coyote newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. Lyman County holds pre-1916 records for this area. The county's small population means local records are limited — regional newspapers and SDSHS collections supplement local holdings. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed county records. The I-90 corridor (formerly US Highway 16) has been the county's transportation lifeline since the auto era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Hanson County. De Smet is the setting for Laura Ingalls Wilder's 'Little House on the Prairie' books. Named for George Kingsbury, territorial historian.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kingsbury County Register of Deeds in De Smet holds deed records from 1873. De Smet is the famous setting of Laura Ingalls Wilder's Little House books — the Laura Ingalls Wilder Memorial Society in De Smet has historical records and the Ingalls homestead site attracts researchers. BLM GLO homestead records document original entries, including the Ingalls family claim. Kingsbury County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Scandinavian (Norwegian) and Anglo-American settlers predominated. The De Smet News newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1880 census captures early settlement. Wilder's books provide detailed descriptions of pioneer life that supplement official records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Hanson and Brookings counties. Named for the numerous lakes in the county. Madison is home to Dakota State University.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lake County Register of Deeds in Madison holds deed records from 1873. Dakota State University in Madison has local collections in its Karl E. Mundt Library. BLM GLO homestead records document original land entries. Lake County Clerk of Courts in Madison has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Norwegian and German settlers predominated — Lutheran church records are essential. The Madison Daily Leader newspaper (1880+) is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county's numerous lakes attracted early settlement and later resort development. Prairie Village near Madison preserves pioneer-era buildings and artifacts. Madison was a railroad town; check railroad land records at the Newberry Library (Chicago).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 during the Black Hills gold rush. Named for John Lawrence, Dakota Territory legislator. Deadwood and Lead were famous mining towns. Home to the Homestake Gold Mine.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lawrence County Register of Deeds in Deadwood holds deed and mining claim records from 1875 — among the most historically significant in SD. Deadwood was one of the most famous mining towns in the American West — saloon licenses, mining claims, and court records document Wild Bill Hickok, Calamity Jane, and thousands of miners. The Adams Museum in Deadwood has extensive local history collections. The Homestake Gold Mine in Lead (1877-2002) was the largest and deepest gold mine in North America — company employment records at SDSHS and the Homestake Adams Research and Cultural Center identify miners from around the world. BLM GLO mineral patent records are essential. Lawrence County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and criminal records from the lawless early days. The Black Hills Pioneer newspaper (1876+) at SDSHS is a premier source. Chinese community records document the significant Chinese population in Deadwood's early years. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1875 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1875. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.
